idea.topic: How a 20-line eval harness caught a bug three senior engineers missed
idea.take: Evals aren't QA you bolt on at the end — they're the spec. Write the eval first and it tells you what to build.
idea.scene: Friday, 5pm. Our support bot calmly told a customer to delete their production database to 'clear the cache.' Nobody caught it in review — the answer read clean.
idea.number: The harness flagged 14% of answers as confidently wrong. Our 200-case manual QA had caught 0% of them.
idea.lesson: Reviewers grade fluency, not truth. A graded eval grades truth — and that gap is exactly where the worst bugs live.
idea.only_you: I spent six years as an air-traffic controller before I wrote a line of code. There, a confident-but-wrong call gets people killed. So I built the harness like an incident review, not a test suite.
idea.mechanism: Every answer gets scored by a second model against hard gates (is there a citation? does it contradict the docs?) plus a 0-10 quality score. Gates fail loud; the score is the dial you watch.
proof: before/after: 14% confidently-wrong dropped to 2% in one week
idea.close: We stopped asking 'does it sound right?' and started asking 'can it prove it?' Ship the eval first.

docs.resume: Applied-AI engineer. Six years as an air-traffic controller (FAA) before switching to software at 29. Self-taught; three years building LLM features for a high-traffic SaaS support product. Ships eval-driven, not demo-driven.

typed.identity: Applied-AI engineer who ships eval-driven LLM features.
typed.known_for: Turning fuzzy 'make the AI better' asks into measurable gates.
typed.background: Six years as an air-traffic controller before switching to software at 29.
typed.beliefs: Most AI 'quality' problems are eval problems wearing a costume. Vibes don't scale; rubrics do.
typed.lessons: Shipped a chatbot once with no evals. It invented a refund policy and cost us a $40k weekend. Never again.

answers.What did you do last weekend?: Rebuilt my bike's brakes on the kitchen floor, got grease on everything, then rode it down the steepest hill near me to test them. Held. Mostly.
answers.Whose posts make you cringe?: The 'thrilled to announce' crowd. Ten adjectives, zero numbers. Just tell me what broke and what you learned from it.
answers.Teach me something you know well: Right of way at an uncontrolled intersection isn't first-come. Whoever's on the right wins the tie. Most drivers guess, and most guess wrong.

writing_samples: We shipped the eval harness today. Twenty lines. It already caught a confidently-wrong answer that three of us missed in review. The lesson isn't 'AI is risky.' The lesson is reviewers grade fluency, not truth. Build the thing that grades truth.
voice.look: short paragraphs, no emojis, the occasional one-line punch
voice.sentence_length: short and varied; fragments allowed for emphasis
voice.notes: Writes like she talks: plain, fast, no warm-up.

audience.writing_for: Builders shipping AI features — engineers and technical founders.
audience.goal: Be known for eval-driven rigor; attract teams who value measurement over demos.
audience.play_to: Respect for proof and specifics; an allergy to hype.

output.format: hook, short body, one takeaway line
